specification.topic-statement: The control socket client sends JSON command requests over a Unix domain socket connection and reads JSON responses, providing programmatic access to the running ikigai process.

scope.in-scope: Connection establishment, request serialization, response buffering for three command types, and connection teardown.
scope.boundary: The caller supplies the socket path explicitly. The client does not discover the path from the environment, from a file, or from the process tree.
Command dispatch, response generation, and the server-side command handlers are covered by the control socket server specification.

field.name: type
field.note: command name; one of read_framebuffer, send_keys, read_token_cache

field.name: keys
field.note: send_keys only; key string injected directly without JSON escaping

field.name: content
field.note: server response, newline-terminated; structure is command-specific

behavior.connection:
A Unix domain stream socket is created and connected to the provided path. If the socket path exceeds the operating system's maximum allowed length, the socket is closed and an error is returned. If the connection attempt fails, the socket is closed and an error is returned.
The returned file descriptor is the sole identifier for the connection. All subsequent operations require the caller to pass this descriptor.
behavior.protocol:
Communication uses line-delimited JSON. Each request is a JSON object serialized as a single line ending with a newline character. Each response is a JSON object serialized as a single line ending with a newline character. There are no length-prefix headers.
The client sends one request and then reads one response before the exchange is complete. There is no multiplexing and no validation that responses correspond to specific requests.
behavior.read-framebuffer-command: A request of type "read_framebuffer" is serialized and sent. The response is read into a 65536-byte buffer using a read loop. Each read operation's result is checked for a newline byte; when a newline is found in the most recently received chunk, the loop exits. The loop also exits when the buffer is full. The buffered response string is returned to the caller.
behavior.send-keys-command:
A request of type "send_keys" is constructed by inserting the key string directly into a JSON template. The key string is not JSON-escaped; if it contains characters that are special in JSON (quotation marks, backslashes, newlines), the resulting JSON is malformed.
After sending, a single read of up to 4096 bytes is performed on a stack-allocated buffer. The response is not returned to the caller. Instead, the response buffer is scanned for the substring "error"; if found, an error is returned. Otherwise, success is returned.
The key string is embedded verbatim into the JSON request. Callers are responsible for ensuring that the key string is valid within a JSON string context, or for accepting undefined behavior on malformed output.
Unlike the other commands, send_keys reads the response with a single system call rather than a loop. If the server sends a response longer than 4096 bytes, the remainder is left in the socket buffer and will corrupt subsequent reads.
behavior.read-token-cache-command: A request of type "read_token_cache" is serialized and sent. The response is read into a 4096-byte buffer using the same loop strategy as read_framebuffer: reading until a newline is detected in the last chunk or the buffer is full. The buffered response string is returned to the caller.
behavior.disconnection: The socket connection is closed by passing the file descriptor to the disconnect operation. Calling disconnect with a negative file descriptor is a no-op.
behavior.error-behavior:
Connection failures, send failures, and receive failures all produce IO errors. Partial writes are not detected; if the operating system sends fewer bytes than requested, the protocol is silently violated and the server will receive a malformed request. End-of-file on a receive loop causes the loop to exit with whatever bytes were received, returning that partial content as the response without error.
Write system calls are not checked for partial completion. On a busy or slow system, a write may deliver fewer bytes than the full request, leaving the server to parse an incomplete JSON line.
